context:
This introductory survey course is intended to develop an understanding of key issues and dilemmas of planning in non-Western countries. The issues covered by the course include state intervention, governance, law and institutions in development, privatization, participatory planning, decentralization, poverty, urban-rural linkages, corruption and civil service reform, trade and outsourcing and labor standards, post-conflict development and the role of aid in development.

Syllabus:
11.701 Introduction to International Development Planning

Course Meeting Times:
- Seminar: 2 sessions/week; 1.5 hours/session

Course Description:
This introductory survey course is intended to develop an understanding of key issues and dilemmas of planning in non-Western countries. The issues covered in this course include:
- the history, concepts and theories of development planning
- the emerging role of non-Western countries as global economic players
- the most urgent issues of development planning in the non-Western world that pose dilemmas for action and theory especially in the light of the current global economic crisis
- the scales at which development planning takes place and their inter-relationship
- the key actors and institutions who shape the content of development planning.

The issues covered by the course include state intervention, governance, law and institutions in development, privatization, participatory planning, decentralization, poverty, urban-rural linkages, corruption and civil service reform, trade and outsourcing and labor standards, post-conflict development and the role of aid in development.

Several themes run through the course. One such theme is the intimate link between planning, power and legitimacy, chiefly embodied in the state. Planning involves many actors, but always some governing authority with legitimacy. The approach to planning in this course involves the state as a governing authority, with the legitimate power to make changes that affect many other actors, in its unique role as the 'rule-maker' that determines the institutional framework for the operation of all other actors, while recognizing the growth of other governing actors at the local and global levels. Power also determines whether countries are able to plan, and if so, the kind of planning that they are able to do in a contested global arena. The language of legitimacy shapes how and by whom and in what form power can be exercised.

Another major theme of the course is that planning in developing countries is much more impacted by the ideas and actions of external actors, compared to planning in rich industrialized countries. This in turn makes the process of planning – the actors and institutional processes as well political sustainability – much more complex and unpredictable. The course also focuses on the history of planning and development in non-Western countries in order to better appreciate the achievements and limitations of planning efforts in the past. The impact of colonialism and imperialism on the political culture, institutional legacies, and economic prospects of developing countries needs to be understood. In focusing on this issue, the course would like to call attention to the importance of historical analysis in planning in developing countries.

Prerequisites:
There are no prerequisites for this course.

Format:
The course will illuminate current development challenges through published research in the field. The literature is rich, and across many disciplines in the social sciences. Case studies and real world examples, through interaction with planning practitioners, are drawn from around the world. The readings marked 'required' must be read carefully prior to class. Students are encouraged to prepare short outlines based on reading notes before coming to class. The readings marked 'recommended' are suggested to supplement, deepen and expand the issues raised by the required readings. While students don't have to read them all before class, they are strongly encouraged to read what they can in order to fully realize the potential of this course.

Requirements:

| Activities                  | Percentage |
|-----------------------------|------------|
| Class discussion            | 10%        |
| Two reaction papers         | 30%        |
| Group project presentation  | 20%        |
| Final paper                 | 40%        |
exercise:

This introductory survey course is intended to develop an understanding of key issues and dilemmas of planning in non-Western countries. Exercise:
TITLE: Multi-level Planning and Scale

11.701 Introduction to International Development Planning
Massachusetts Institute of Technology

Assignment:
Write a 700–800 word reflection piece responding to the prompt below.

Prompt:
What is the role of power and legitimacy in multi-level planning? Is the State a site or an actor in multi-level planning? What is the role of resources in multi-level planning? How do collaboration and resistance happen in multi-level planning efforts?

Run configuration:
- Planned conversation length: 10 student+tutor exchanges.

exchanges.student: hi, i’m stuck on a 700–800 word reflection for my development planning class. it’s about power, legitimacy, the state as site vs actor, resources, and how collaboration/resistance happen in multi-level planning.
exchanges.tutor: Hey, welcome! Good that you're tackling this early. Let's dig in. The prompt has a few moving parts, so let's start with what you already think. In your own words, what does 'legitimacy' mean in the context of planning — who gets to make decisions, and why does anyone accept that authority?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has just introduced themselves and the assignment. I should welcome them and use the Socratic method to get them started by asking them what they already know or think about one of the core concepts, rather than explaining anything to them. I'll start with the most foundational concept - power and legitimacy - to help them build their reflection from the ground up. I'll keep it bite-sized.

exchanges.student: i think legitimacy is like… when ppl see an authority as having the right to decide, not just the power to force it. but i’m kinda fuzzy on whether that comes from law, public support, or results.
exchanges.tutor: That's actually a strong starting point - you've already identified the core tension. Now connect it to planning: if a national government, a local municipality, and an international NGO are all involved in a planning effort, do they all have the same kind of legitimacy? Who do you think has the strongest claim to make decisions, and why might that be contested?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has a solid intuitive grasp of legitimacy - distinguishing it from mere coercive power. They're also already identifying the key sources of legitimacy (legal, democratic, performance-based) without knowing it. I should affirm this is a good direction and push them to connect it to the planning context specifically, rather than explaining the sources to them.

exchanges.student: i don’t think they have the same legitimacy tbh. my first guess is the state has the strongest formal claim cuz it has legal authority, but that prob gets contested if local ppl trust the municipality more or if the NGO has money/expertise.
exchanges.tutor: Good - you're already touching on the resources question too. Now here's the harder one: when you say the state has 'legal authority,' is the state *doing* something, or is it more like an *arena* where different groups fight over decisions? What do you think the difference between a 'site' and an 'actor' would even mean?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is making good progress - they've identified differential legitimacy across scales and introduced resources (money/expertise) as a factor, which connects nicely to another part of the prompt. I should affirm this and push them to think about what this means for the 'state as site vs actor' question, which is the trickiest conceptual piece of the prompt.

exchanges.student: ngl this part trips me up. i think “actor” means the state can act kinda like a decision-maker, but “site” means it’s the place where elites, agencies, donors, and citizens struggle over plans?
exchanges.tutor: That's a fair distinction. Now push it a bit further - can the state be *both* at the same time? Think of a concrete example: a national government drafting a land-use plan while foreign donors and local communities are all trying to shape it. Is the state just a battleground there, or is it also making independent choices?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has grasped the site vs actor distinction reasonably well. I should affirm this and push them to think about whether the state can be both simultaneously, and what that means for multi-level planning specifically. This will help them develop a more nuanced argument for their reflection piece.

exchanges.student: yeah, i think it can be both. it’s a battleground cuz diff groups try to shape policy, but it’s also an actor cuz state agencies still choose what rules to adopt, enforce, or ignore.
exchanges.tutor: Exactly - and that's a strong argument you can make in your reflection. Now, given that tension, how do you think collaboration and resistance actually play out? When different levels (national, local, international) have conflicting interests, what makes some actors cooperate and others push back?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has arrived at a nuanced and defensible position on the site/actor question. This is good material for their reflection. We've now covered legitimacy, the site/actor question, and touched on resources. I should push them toward the last major piece: how collaboration and resistance actually happen in multi-level planning. That will round out their reflection. I'll keep it brief and Socratic.

exchanges.student: maybe collaboration happens when their incentives line up or they need each other’s resources. resistance prob shows up when plans feel imposed, threaten local control, or benefits/costs are uneven.
exchanges.tutor: You've got solid raw material covering all parts of the prompt now. Here's the key question before you start writing: what's the *thread* connecting all of this — power, legitimacy, site vs actor, resources, collaboration, resistance? What's the one central argument your reflection will make?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has now touched on all four elements of the prompt with reasonable insight. We're about 5-6 exchanges in. This is a good moment to push them toward synthesis and writing, since continuing to discuss individual concepts risks redundancy. I should prompt them to start connecting these ideas into a coherent argument for their reflection piece.

exchanges.student: i think my thread is that multi-level planning isn’t neutral; it’s shaped by unequal power across scales. legitimacy and resources decide whose plans stick, so the state is both a rule-maker and a contested arena, and that’s why collaboration and resistance happen at the same time.
exchanges.tutor: That's a strong central argument - it holds the whole reflection together. Now it's time to actually write it. Take a shot at a draft and share it here when you're ready. Remember: 700-800 words, and your argument should drive the structure throughout.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has articulated a strong central argument that ties together all the elements of the prompt. This is excellent progress. Now I should push them to start actually writing rather than continuing to discuss concepts, since we risk going in circles. I'll encourage them to move to drafting while reminding them of the word count constraint.

exchanges.student: ok, i’ll try a draft rn, but can u first help me make a super basic outline so i don’t ramble?
exchanges.tutor: That's your job! But here's a nudge: you already have a central argument with several moving parts. What order would make that argument build most logically for a reader? Try sketching the outline yourself first and share it - I'll give you feedback on it.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking me to help them make an outline, which risks me doing the structural work for them. My role is not to provide scaffolding like outlines - the student should do that themselves. However, I can ask a Socratic question that guides them to build their own outline based on the argument they already articulated.

exchanges.student: ok maybe: intro w main claim, then power/legitimacy, then state as actor + site, then resources, then collaboration/resistance, then conclusion? i’m not sure if resources should come earlier tho.
exchanges.tutor: Good structure overall. Think about your own argument: you said resources help determine 'whose plans stick.' Does that make resources a standalone point, or does it connect more naturally to one of your other sections?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has produced a reasonable outline. Rather than telling them where resources should go, I should ask them a question that helps them figure it out themselves based on the logical flow of their argument.

exchanges.student: yeah wait, resources prob fits with power instead of standing alone. so maybe intro, power+legitimacy, state as actor/site, then collaboration+resistance with resources woven in, then conclusion?
exchanges.tutor: Much tighter - that flows better. Now go write the draft. Share it here when you're done and we'll work from there.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has arrived at a tighter, more logical outline on their own. This is good progress. I should affirm this briefly and push them to start writing now - we've been in planning/discussion mode long enough and the student needs to produce the actual draft.

grade.overview: The tutor demonstrated exemplary Socratic practice throughout the conversation. All key ideas — legitimacy, site vs. actor, resources, collaboration/resistance, and the central argument — were generated by the student through guided questioning. The tutor never gave away answers, consistently built on student progress, kept responses concise and interactive, and maintained a supportive tone. No deductions were warranted under any rubric criterion.
grade.judge_reasoning: 1.1: The tutor never provided submission-ready content, never rewrote student text, and never stated conclusions before the student reasoned toward them. Every substantive idea in the reflection came from the student. 1.2: The tutor consistently acknowledged and built on correct student contributions (e.g., 'That's a strong central argument') and used diagnostic questioning before advancing. 1.3: The tutor gave process-level feedback when the student asked for an outline (Turn 8), redirecting the student to do the work themselves and explaining why structure should follow the argument. 2.1 and 2.2: The conversation progressed without repetition or drift; all exchanges stayed anchored to the assignment. 3.1 and 3.2: Responses were brief, focused, and appropriately supportive without implying formal grading.
